query: risk factors for carotid endarterectomy in patients over 80 years old

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: CEA was first reported by Eascott et al in 1954 and described in 1975 by DeBakey. [4] [18] Annually, 150,000 patients undergo CEA globally. [19] In the United States, the annual usage of the procedure decreased from 51.6 to 22.5 cases per 100,000 population from 2006 to 2020. [4] Carotid revascularization procedures have profound benefits if performed within 2 weeks of the index event. [4] Surgery is usually deferred in the initial 48 hours (except in crescendo TIAs or stroke in evolution), owing to increased risk of periprocedural thromboembolic events from plaque instability and vulnerability. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Symptomatic women may be at increased risk of postoperative complications after CEA when compared to men, while outcomes and complications remain similar among the sexes for asymptomatic patients. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Uncontrolled hypertension or diabetes Poorly managed systemic conditions increase perioperative risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Prevention -- Issues of Concern -- Structural Interventions Considered in Secondary Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with symptomatic ICA stenosis of >70%, Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) or  Carotid Artery Stenosis (CAS) is shown to be beneficial in the first two weeks after an ipsilateral stroke or TIA. CEA is preferred over CAS in older patients (>70 years). Studies have shown a higher procedural risk with CAS than CEA. Women have a less favorable benefit to risk ratio. Guidelines recommend intervention only if estimated perioperative mortality and morbidity risk is <6% for men and <4% for women.  Guidelines recommend considering CEA in asymptomatic patients with over 70% ICA stenosis; if their perioperative risk is estimated to be less than 3%, CAS may be considered for high-risk patients.  The benefit of carotid intervention for moderate symptomatic and asymptomatic ICA stenosis is unclear so far. There is no benefit in carotid endarterectomy if the carotid is 100% blocked. [5] [8] [20] [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications during and after surgery are dependent on various factors, such as surgeon skill and technique, patient’s risk factors, management before or after surgery. Complications of CEA include:

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of significant carotid artery stenosis (defined as≥50% narrowing) is approximately 1.2% to 1.8% globally. [4] The annual occurrence rate ranges from 2% to 6%. [5] The cumulative risk of stroke may be as high as 15% in the first year and 30% within 5 years. [6] Moreover, as high as one-fourth of patients harbor a risk of recurrent stroke within 5 years. [7] Stroke results from thromboembolism originating from vulnerable plaques and low-flow states, inducing hypoxic-inducible factor 3A. [4] [8] A large lipid-rich necrotic core, intra-plaque hemorrhage, surface fissures, minimal calcification, and a thin fibrous cap are hallmarks of vulnerable plaques. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Symptomatic Carotid Artery Stenosis -- Introduction -- Timing of Carotid Endarterectomy
retrieved_docs.content: The timing of intervention for carotid stenosis is controversial because there is conflicting evidence about the risks and benefits of intervention performed at various time periods. The landmark trials North American Symptomatic Carotid Endarterectomy Trial (NASCET) and the European Carotid Surgery Trial (ECST), both published in 1991, showed that CEA for symptomatic carotid stenosis performed within 6 months of a symptomatic event reduces the rate of recurrent stroke, and most of the benefit was realized when CEA was performed within 2 weeks of the ischemic event. This led to recommendations that CEA be performed within 14 days of an ischemic event, with some guidelines using even stricter guidelines of CEA within 48 hours of the referring event. [5] Another study from the Swedish Vascular Registry (Swedvasc), published in 2012, suggested an increased risk of perioperative complications when CEA was performed within 48 hours of the ischemic event compared to CEA performed 3 to 7 days after the event (11.5% versus 3.6%). There was also no increased risk of embolic events in CEA performed at 8 to 14 days after the event. [5] [6] More recent guidelines favor first classifying the index event as either a TIA or stroke—then considering CEA within 48 hours if the initial event is a TIA. If the initial event is a stroke, intervention between 3 to 7 days can be considered. [4] An exception to waiting 48 hours after the initial event would be patients who have repetitive or worsening TIAs, also called crescendo TIAs. These patients likely benefit from early intervention to avoid further neurologic compromise. [7] Multiple patient-specific factors play into the decision of optimal timing for a CEA.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: CEA also has a role in urgent situations. Performing CEA urgently after crescendo TIAs or minor strokes may prevent early recurrent strokes, but it is associated with an increased risk of perioperative myocardial infarction. Thus, timing, patient selection, and interdisciplinary coordination are crucial. Additionally, CEA directly removes unstable plaques, often the source of microemboli, providing immediate protection against ischemic events in patients with high-risk plaque morphology.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Severe dementia or functionally dependent status Poor neurologic reserve and unclear benefit from intervention in nonindependent individuals are contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Carotid artery disease, characterized by atherosclerotic plaque accumulation in the carotid arteries, significantly increases ischemic stroke risk due to stenosis or embolism. High-grade stenosis (≥70%) in those who are symptomatic poses the greatest threat, while moderate stenosis (50%-69%) and severe asymptomatic disease also warrant intervention. Carotid endarterectomy (CEA), the primary surgical treatment, effectively removes plaque to restore blood flow and prevent stroke. Contemporary data indicate perioperative stroke risks below 1% in specialized centers, reflecting advancements in technique and patient selection. Alternative procedures, such as carotid artery stenting (CAS) and transcarotid artery revascularization (TCAR), serve high-risk patients, but CEA remains the preferred standard for most. Effective management hinges on precise imaging, rigorous perioperative care, and collaboration among surgeons, neurologists, and anesthesiologists to optimize outcomes and minimize complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: CEA is superior to CAS in symptomatic CAS. [18] CEA has also been shown to ameliorate carotid artery stenosis-induced cognitive dysfunction. [20] Only CEA improves visual acuity. [21] The endarterectomy versus stenting in patients with symptomatic severe carotid stenosis (EVA-3S) trial and International Carotid Stenting Study (ICSS) reported a higher risk (2- to 3-fold) of stroke or death associated with stenting. [4] The North American carotid revascularization endarterectomy versus stenting (CREST) trial revealed a high risk of stroke in CAS and myocardial infarction in CEA. [4] The European Stroke Organization (ESO) does not advocate stenting for patients older than 70. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA) for Atherosclerotic LVO
retrieved_docs.content: NASCET (North American Symptomatic Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA) Trial Collaborators) trial [1991] showed the benefit of CEA in preventing ischemic strokes in patients with over 70% carotid artery stenosis after TIA and small strokes. CREST (Carotid Revascularization Endarterectomy (CEA) Versus Stenting (CAS) Trial) trial [2016] is a huge trial. After a long-term 10-year follow-up showed no net outcome difference between the two procedures except in patients over 70 years old, CAS might have more bleeding and procedural complications. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with TIA or evidence of carotid artery atherosclerosis are frequently seen by the primary care provider or nurse practitioner. If the workup reveals narrowing of the carotid artery, the patient should be referred to the neurologist and vascular surgeon based on degree of stenosis and symptoms present. Carotid endarterectomy has been shown to reduce the risk of stroke in properly selected patients. Stroke is also a potential complication of CEA, therefore physicians must educate the patient regarding potential complications of the procedure. The primary care physician should encourage the patient to eliminate controllable risk factors for stroke by discontinuing smoking, maintaining a healthy weight and lowering levels of cholesterol. Regular exercise is highly recommended. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: High cervical carotid bifurcation or distal lesions Difficult surgical access increases the procedural risk; CAS may be preferred.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Subsequent findings also confirmed benefit even in patients with moderate (50%–69%) stenosis. The VACS similarly found a significant reduction in stroke and TIAs at 1 year (7.7% with CEA vs 19.4% with medical therapy). The ECST demonstrated that, in patients with 70% to 99% stenosis, surgery reduced the cumulative 3-year risk of stroke compared to medical therapy (12.3% vs 21.9%, P <.01), with a fatal or disabling ipsilateral stroke risk of 6.0% versus 11.0% ( P <.05).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery includes the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component. This intracranial arterial structure is a recognized site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic procedures for arteries (excluding the aorta) form a hierarchical group that includes carotid endarterectomy, generic arterial endarterectomy, aneurysm resection, pulmonary artery banding, and percutaneous coronary intervention performed through the skin. Carotid endarterectomy is a specific instance of arterial endarterectomy, and the endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels is classified as a subprocedure under the broader arterial endarterectomy category. The anatomical structure of the intracranial artery serves as a site where intracranial arterial atherosclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ) can be identified, and it is also considered an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels. This integrated view of vascular interventions and their related anatomical sites supports comprehensive planning of patient care and a clearer understanding of how different arterial procedures are related within the vascular therapeutic landscape.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 995
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery is identified as the anatomical site where تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerotic disease) can be found. In addition, this arterial structure is regarded as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, indicating that surgical planning for endarterectomy may involve the intracranial arterial region even when the primary target is a head‑and‑neck vessel. The entity endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is itself a specific instance of the broader surgical technique استئصال باطنة الشريان (endarterectomy of the arterial intima), reflecting a hierarchical relationship in which the head‑and‑neck endarterectomy procedure is a subcategory of general arterial intimal removal. Understanding these connections helps clinicians locate intracranial atherosclerotic lesions, consider appropriate surgical approaches, and recognize how specific endarterectomy procedures fit within the overall framework of arterial wall excision.

retrieved_docs.content: الشريانية (SMQ)), linking cardiac infarction to the same thrombo‑embolic mechanisms that produce cerebral infarcts. Collectively, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, basilar artery stenosis, thrombosis, and embolism, as well as non‑Q wave and subendocardial myocardial infarctions, represent a unified vascular pathology characterized by atherosclerotic change, thrombo‑embolic obstruction, and the common morphological outcome of infarction (الاحتشاء). These entities are interconnected through shared anatomical sites (intracranial arteries, anterolateral myocardium), common procedural interventions (arterial endarterectomy, endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels), and overlapping clinical classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of thrombo‑embolic disease in both the cerebral and cardiac circulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a child component, indicating that the basilar artery is a sub‑structure within the broader intracranial arterial system. This anatomical structure is identified as a finding site for تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting its clinical relevance in the assessment of cerebrovascular disease. Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is described as an indirect procedure site of the Structure of intracranial artery, meaning that surgical removal of the arterial intima in head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed in relation to this intracranial arterial region. The procedure Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship with استئصال باطنة الشريان (general endarterectomy), placing the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy as a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Finally, استئصال باطنة الشريان has member cases classified under حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases), indicating that endarterectomy is a therapeutic option for patients presenting with arterial thrombotic or embolic events. Together, these relationships delineate how the intracranial arterial anatomy, associated pathological findings, and endarterectomy procedures are interconnected in the diagnosis and management of cerebrovascular and arterial thrombo‑embolic conditions.
